feedback.text: Thank you for sharing your feedback! I would like to suggest that the Digital Fairness Act also covers digitally purchased products such as video games. There has been a worrying trend in industry in recent years: Video games are often developed and sold with a particular focus on online features, but as end-users they tend to play only a so-called ‘licence’. This is mostly hidden in any end-user licensing agreement (usually ‘EULA’ in industry). In these EULAs, game manufacturers often assume any responsibility to offer the online services at all and often take up the freedom to withdraw the end-user’s ‘licence’ at any time for any reason (i.e.: make the purchased and paid product unplayable). An example that affected me personally is the video game ‘Splatoon’ for the WiiU (2015 release year). In this colourful video game, a strong focus is exclusively on the multi-player aspect. However, this was made inaccessible after 8 years in January 2023 because the operator Nintendo switched off the online services. As a result, the core of the paid product has subsequently become inoperable. It is good, it is understandable that a company does not want to continue operating online services forever – renting or operating computer servers costs money. There is therefore a financial interest for such products. However, what is incomprehensible to me is why the online functionality of digital products can be deleted without replacement. There are technologies that make it possible to continue playing a game online at no continuous cost, such as peer 2 peer connectivity. It connects each other and communicates directly via the users’ internet connection – without Nintendo server as a middleman (i.e. no longer running costs). Peer 2 peers have even become industry standard in the early times of online gambling. (For example: Age of Empires 2 [1999], Terraria [2011], Quake [1996]) There was even at this time a cultural phenomenon that was only possible thanks to this connectivity: The LAN Party (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/LAN_party). Today, peer 2 peer connectivity is becoming less and less available in industry. I suppose that there is even a lack of financial interest here: By simply making old products inaccessible, end-users can be forced to adapt the new product. Nintendos ‘Splatoon 2’ and ‘Splatoon 3’ for the newer game console, the switch. Why should Nintendo still want to make the old first splatoon playable at all if this could mean fewer players (consumers) for the successors? So if you want to play a Multiplayer Splatoon again, only two options remain: Either buying the new product pays for the new online service OR trusts that technology-converted hardcore fans host their own server as if they were the official Nintendo server. This second route is usually not an option for the average end-user, who is only partially tech-affined. Moreover, Nintendo is often aggressive against such do-it-yourself projects. Supporters regularly remove DIY projects from the internet under threat of legal action. Here I could list a number of examples, but I think the best example is the ‘#FreeMelee’ debacle. Content: In 2020, players wanted to play a 19-year-old game in a tournament and live stream it online. However, due to the coronavirus pandemic, there was no presence. The players therefore resorted to a specially modified game version (which offered an online game) – the event was quickly hit with a ‘Cease & desist’ threat. Thank you for your time.
